content:
> Well, this has been a long journey! We've completed all the prerequisite work, and even fixed two items from the [post-deprecation list](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/145954).

@yjbanov Thanks for your and everyone else's work! I think the link might be wrong here: was https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/153974 intended for the post-deprecation list?

That is great news that some progress has been made to identify the crashes on Canvaskit on mobile around image management here: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/93404 It would be really great if that can be resolved before removing the HTML renderer so those of us who need it for a stable Mobile Web App will not be left behind on an older version.

Hello @yjbanov ,
We have stumbled upon https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/159974 blocker issue in Canvaskit renderer, while utilising Element Embeding scenario in mobile phones.
This is impacting the viability of our project and we cannot transition to Canvaskit without this being fixed.
Will it be possible to build chrome extensions using Flutter web without HTML renderer? AFAIK, it uses the HTML renderer...
@BirjuVachhani yes, please see the above: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/145954#issuecomment-2486162109

There is also an existing limitation for the number of active `HtmlElementView` in CanvasKit. for instance we need this to display user cameras, each user needs a Html `VideoElement` and thus an `HtmlElementView` to display and align it properly. See issue #113699 for more details.

@yjbanov @harryterkelsen I could not get https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/157755 to fix CORS issues on the web. See [my comment](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/157755#issuecomment-2525409863) there. Could be a simple issue on my end, but if not, it feels like a serious issue.
@Levi-Lesches if CORS is still not working, can you please file a new issue with clear repro steps?

I'd like to share some issues that have arisen due to the removal of the HTML renderer and the iOS 18.2 scroll issue not being included in the 3.24.x. This is to inform, not criticize. I am writing to share what happened and discuss improvements.

In iOS 18.2, users encountered a problem where they could not scroll in Flutter web application.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/158299

iOS 18.2 was released on December 11, 2024, making this problem widespread among users.

https://developer.apple.com/documentation/ios-ipados-release-notes/ios-ipados-18_2-release-notes

The issue was fixed on November 20, 2024.

https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/56719

However, this fix has not been cherry-picked into the 3.24.x.

https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/56903

Because of the above, we need to update to Flutter 3.27.x.

---

Update: This block is a misunderstanding by @koji-1009. the HTML renderer is still available in 3.27.0.

In Flutter 3.27, the HTML renderer is no longer available. We will need to migrate from the HTML renderer to CanvasKit.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/156376
https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/159314

---

The combination of iPhone + Safari + CanvasKit is prone to application crashes and our application needs to be checked and fixed to work. Since there are many iPhone users, this is seen as a serious problem.
However, CanvasKit also has problems. For example, there is a problem with Image.network where the resize option is not available, but no stable fix has been distributed yet.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/160199

---

We're thinking of using the master branch commit to fix the HTML renderer issue in iOS 18.2 because switching to CanvasKit will take time.

But right now, the big issue is not only technical.
The big concern now is the need to discuss the future of Flutter Web within the team. The developers believe in Flutter web, but the trust of the team members is losing. If trust continues to lose, we will have to choose a technology that is not Flutter web.

I understand the interest in promoting CanvasKit and the advantages of moving to it.
However, just because the HTML renderer is removed doesn't mean that all Flutter web applications can be easily migrated to CanvasKit. Could we support the Flutter Web migration by temporarily reverting to removing the HTML renderer? Or is it possible to have a version available as 3.24.6 that resolves the iOS 18.2 issue?

Also, is there any way to make Flutter web releases more incremental? For example, if Flutter web crashes in a beta version of Safari, Firefox, or Chrome, could we prioritize distributing it on the current rather than releasing the next stable version?

Thank you for reading this message.
I faced with that issue too

[edit] Thanks for clarifying @yjbanov, glad the html renderer still is available [/edit]

Same here. For us it's very unfortunate that the html-renderer was killed already before system emoji support has landed for CanvasKit. Now we are forced to upgrade in a very busy period before holidays and add a significant size to our bundle because of the Noto Color Emoji font whilst getting a worse UX for iOS/Mac users.

@yjbanov 
Thanks for the info. And sorry, I was wrong about the HTML renderer not working with Flutter 3.27.0.

On the other hand, the information you provide reads as a suggestion to apply the updated version immediately after the release of the Flutter SDK. Since the release date of the Flutter SDK is not made public in advance, it is necessary for developers to hurry up and plan for the update when the Flutter SDK is released. Since this issue affects the planning of all team members, not just the developers, this situation raises the following question: “Why can't we plan for updates in advance if we choose Flutter Web?”

And the biggest question currently being asked is, “The next time there is a problem with Flutter Web not working in a beta version of the browser, can it be fixed before the stable version is distributed?” It is the first topic developers have to answer to our team members. We need to clarify if we can resolve similar issues with (for example) Safari before the next release of the Flutter SDK. Even if the next stable release (2025 first release) is coming soon, could we still prioritize cherry-picking changes to the current stable release (3.27.x)?

@koji-1009 @yjbanov 
>> The next time there is a problem with Flutter Web not working in a beta version of the browser, can it be fixed before the stable version is distributed?

IMO, if Flutter Web "stopped" working in beta of any major browser, the latest two Flutter versions must immediately be fixed.
In that case versions **3.22 and 3.24**.

Could someone please explain the naming of the new web renderers to me? Why does the documentation call them **default** and **WebAssembly**? To me it looks like all new renderers are WebAssembly based? 

There is
- `canvaskit.wasm`
- `chromium/canvaskit.wasm`
- `skwasm.wasm`

Isn't `.wasm` the WebAssembly extension?

Also, please make sure to **not** repeat issue #96509. A user picking the `--no-wasm` option does not want the additional garbage collected stuff...

i am having a problem with CanvasKit , it does not show pictures fetched from links (google profile pictures per exemple) and the view gets all messed up, see picture for more comprehension. the error that i am getting is : SecurityError: Failed to execute 'texImage2D' on 'WebGL2RenderingContext': The image element contains cross-origin data, and may not be loaded. I need a solution please!

> i am having a problem with CanvasKit , it does not show pictures fetched from links (google profile pictures per exemple) and the view gets all messed up, see picture for more comprehension. the error that i am getting is : SecurityError: Failed to execute 'texImage2D' on 'WebGL2RenderingContext': The image element contains cross-origin data, and may not be loaded. I need a solution please!

This issue isn't the place for individual debugging, but just to highlight something that's mentioned in the original post: this is related to something called CORS. Hopefully https://docs.flutter.dev/platform-integration/web/web-images can help you.
Did anyone successfully write E2E tests using canvaskit/wasm? I.e no html elements, I find it impossible since the locators in e.g playwright cant find elements to click on.

@Juliusvm 
You can use semantics for finding and testing, it works for both html and canvaskit renderers, take a look: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/97455#issuecomment-1058758602

@Juliusvm 

What @slavap said, and this was also discussed above in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/145954#issuecomment-2138419510.

> i am having a problem with CanvasKit , it does not show pictures fetched from links (google profile pictures per exemple) and the view gets all messed up, see picture for more comprehension. the error that i am getting is : SecurityError: Failed to execute 'texImage2D' on 'WebGL2RenderingContext': The image element contains cross-origin data, and may not be loaded. I need a solution please!

@AbderrazakBenarous Have you found a solution? I have the same issue after upgrading to Flutter 3.27.0. Images show up only if I use HTML render.
@timbera Does this happen on the master channel? This might have been https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/160127. I want to say the fix is landing in 3.27.2 🤞

Quite a thread.  I want to hop back to something mentioned early on here:

> On the native side Flutter already requires a GPU, and with Impeller that requirement will go even further and require the latest crop of graphics APIs that don't make sense without GPUs at all. I doubt that Flutter Web is positioned to fight that trend or risk putting brakes on Flutter overall. I think if GPU-less hardware is important for your app, Flutter may not be the right choice

This is interesting.  I do hear the reasoning. But there are a lot of devices out there without GPUs, still.  We work with schools from the district level down to the classroom level, and while we were originally planning on a mobile-only product we quickly found that:

- Chromebooks tend to be the default device in the majority of classrooms (in the US at least -- internationally we tend to work more with independent schools, which tend to have a different demographic)
- Recent trends have led to _fewer_ students having access to a mobile device at school, and driven _up_ chromebook usage
- Many chromebooks, especially those sold in bulk to schools/districts, _do not have GPUs_

I can see how the easier path is to ***shrug*** and move on.  And maybe this is the better path for Flutter -- progress first!  But one of the promises of the web is accessibility -- and that's one of my biggest nits with the design doc.  The HTML renderer _is_ much more accessible than CanvasKit, just in a different way than described in the doc.  It's accessible to millions of more users who are working with, for whatever reasons, lower power devices.

I'm glad I'm not in your shoes.  The limitations of the HTML renderer (although they mostly seem to be focused on drawing to the canvas), are well-documented.  The open issues with CanvasKit are also moderately terrifying.

Ignoring the GPU-only support challenge; I think fixing the CanvasKit issues (at the very least, the ones in #153974, but it might be worth tracking all the ones in this thread) _before_ removing the HTML renderer would go a long way towards making people more comfortable with this process.  I'm sure most of the participants here have had some experience with issues languishing in the tracker (no shade -- that's just the nature of ambitious projects with a multi platform/device footprint).

Anyway, all this makes me a bit nervous.  As our team is committed to supporting schools, and not just the schools that can afford devices with dedicated GPUs, I'm not sure what our path forward looks like.  

We might just end up stuck on `3.27`.

Thanks for all the time and work put into this framework and for listening to all the feedback in this thread :)

I am scratching my head in disbelief that this is even being considered. I understand the need to focus on the next generation(s) of content delivery into browsers, but to remove the support for the delivery of the bare bones of the web (i.e. HTML) is astonishing. In the past I have needed to embed functionality (such as a third party WebRTC client into a frame) manually. Heaven knows how I would achieve this without being able to inspect the output, when I have no control on what is provided by a third party. 

I have also previously had to retrograde canvaskit builds back to html in some projects when certain packages are not performing as expected - in one case the rendering of SVGs creating a clunky experience in non-chrome browsers hence the need to manage them manually at html level.

The more I think about this, the more I feel like if Flutter goes down this route (which seems to be happening), this should be acknowledged front and center.

From the homepage (additions italicized):
> Flutter transforms the development process. Build, test, and deploy beautiful mobile, ~~web,~~ desktop, and embedded experiences from a single codebase.  _Flutter also works on the web on modern devices that have dedicated GPUs._

Mostly because when people think "oh, this compiles to the web?" the default assumption is _accessibility_, because that's what the web is for.

While the update/upgrade cycle for mobile devices is common, when computers get old?  Well, you can still browse the web :)

@wreppun 
>> when computers get old? Well, you can still browse the web :)

wasm + canvaskit renderer works faster on the old PCs than js + html renderer :-)

accessibility - if you are talking about the real accessibility - does not depend on renderer, works for html and canvaskit renderers.

Hello,
Please add https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/84288 in the backlog of the mandatory issues to resolve before deprecating Html renderer. Otherwise, millions of Chrome Extension Based Flutter apps will no longer work.

It does not seem to be a very intricate issue to resolve, the https://www.gstatic.com/flutter-canvaskit/ script should be downloaded in the build folder at build time, not loaded externally and ran using eval which is forbidden in Chrome Extension.

@tolotrasamuel : "millions of Chrome Extension Based Flutter apps will no longer work" - millions?

"should be downloaded in the build folder at build time" – have you seen the `--no-web-resources-cdn` flag? We already support this.

@kevmoo  Can we get a formal "don't plan on SEO now/anytime/ever" or "we have a an SEO solution in the works" or "we are actively seeking solutions as we know this is important"?
thanks

canvasKit is bloated and has some major issues such as CORS, unable to work with any web automation test suite and much much more. It is just unusable for any production application. HTML renderer even with all its short comings is, at a minimum, a first class citizen of the web. It produces DOM (even if shadow DOM), has smaller download size and doesn't have weird CORS issues. It is a terrible decision to drop support for HTML renderer, if anything the Flutter team should be working on improving it.

@yjbanov 

When rendering WebRTC video using HtmlElementView with CanvasKit,
it consumes a significant amount of resources, especially on low-end devices and Chromebooks. 
Therefore, we are still sticking with the HTML renderer. We need a solution for this issue.
Hi, you should try rendering in CanvasKit but remove the CachedNetworkImage and use Image.Network() only for the web. Might be some caching conflicts with the browser. Worked for me 🙏

> [@yjbanov](https://github.com/yjbanov)
> 
> When rendering WebRTC video using HtmlElementView with CanvasKit, it consumes a significant amount of resources, especially on low-end devices and Chromebooks. Therefore, we are still sticking with the HTML renderer. We need a solution for this issue.

Similar issue for us. We are using a map platform view and the rendering performance with Canvaskit is significantly slower compared to the html renderer.

@parancode @felix-mittermeier 

Can you please file issues with repro steps? We're optimizing exactly this aspect right now.
@parancode @felix-mittermeier – we've created https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/162618 to track these concerns.
Regarding CORS, I think it might be possible to make the use_img_tag flag in the Image widget.

My reasons for including web-rendered html: Quicker site launch, adequate site display and smiley support. If at least the startup speed became the same as with html, I think it makes sense to remove render html
Html renderer is removed from flutter 3.29, any workaround to use html renderer on 3.29? I need the load speed of html renderer and new features on flutter 3.29
@elliotkhd I see nothing in 3.29 release notes about removal of html renderer, only removal from docs.

See https://medium.com/flutter/whats-new-in-flutter-3-29-f90c380c2317 "Removal of the web HTML renderer"

See also https://groups.google.com/g/flutter-announce/c/fngDlrI-nvY from Dec 11
And https://groups.google.com/g/flutter-announce/c/JqkMe7cPkQo/m/Jx5VhNVOAwAJ from Aug 20
do not remove html render.   canvaskit makes web crash badly in mobile safari especially when  there are many pictures in the web.  or anyone can solves this problem?

> do not remove html render. canvaskit makes web crash badly in mobile safari especially when there are many pictures in the web. or anyone can solves this problem?

We've done a BUNCH of work to improve this in the latest release. Please try it and file issues. CC @harryterkelsen

@kevmoo hope you're doing well! `NetworkImage(FirebaseAuth.instance.currentUser!.photoURL!)` throws a 429 exception on web when using defaults, but is fixed when using `--web-renderer=html` even though it gives a deprecation warning. So I think some fixes are still needed

Flutter team web solution is so bad , even after making it stable for 3 year and many basic things are very poor , flutter web is still looking like in alpha stage. load time , SEO , performance , network image ..... hope at least from skwasm future version will give decent improvements

> [...] `NetworkImage(FirebaseAuth.instance.currentUser!.photoURL!)` throws a 429 exception on web when using defaults, but is fixed when using `--web-renderer=html` even though it gives a deprecation warning. So I think some fixes are still needed

@saurabhguptasg I recommend to open an issue for that (complete with reproducible code sample)

"Massive issues? Just consider the load time alone. I’ve been a strong advocate for Flutter, even convincing two of my past companies to choose it over RN. Acknowledging Flutter Web’s limitations isn’t bashing—it’s recognizing areas that need improvement while still hoping for progress.

SEO not being a 'use case' isn’t an excuse—it’s simply a challenge the team hasn’t solved yet

After 3 to 4 year of stable I felt few things on mobile browser are
 
Startup performance: Even simple apps take significantly longer to load compared to native web frameworks. The massive JS bundle size 

Network image handling: Flutter Web still struggles with caching, responsiveness, and loading performance for images, making UIs feel sluggish.

Text rendering issues: The way text is handled in the CanvasKit/WebGL model often leads to blurry text, incorrect spacing, and accessibility problems.

Keyboard and input inconsistencies: Handling focus, text input, and accessibility across different browsers is still unreliable.
Scrolling performance: It’s not as smooth as native web or RN web, especially on complex UIs. Mouse wheel behavior and trackpad gestures often feel unnatural.*

Every time we deliver a project, we get the same client feedback: ‘The mobile app looks great, but the web version feels slow—why?’ That speaks for itself. Flutter Web has improved, but let’s not overlook the real issues that still need work."
**I just wanted to stay on flutter** 

And also when it's canvas render instead of html on mobile it's taking still longer to load . Yes I agrees the way flutter is build make it harder to improve it , but being a **flutter dev I love to see the magic** 
React Native's New Architecture is a significant overhaul of its core systems

I love flutter ❤️

Thanks for your answer @PrathivDev, which clearly indicates I misinterpreted your initial message. My bad!
You point out some valid issues that we have encountered too, but we have always found solutions (sometimes relying on smart bitmap caching, using custom painters to avoid heavy re-layout calculation, ...).

We have literally thousands of reactive rows filled with custom formatting/rich texts/values in multiple massive data grids, with sometimes dozens of multidimensional columns updated in real-time with WebSocket updates. It was challenging to keep a butter-smooth experience (like it would have been in a traditional JS/DOM approach), but having access to low-level rendering/WebGL canvas while keeping the widget hierarchy in the mix was really useful to achieve our results.

For us, keyboard handling is not an issue, and we have a looot of shortcuts for different sub-sections of each running program in the Flutter main app. However, in our very specific project (an internal app), every user is using Chrome only. That avoids a lot of the issues you encounter.

Good luck with your projects and have a nice day!
Flutter powa!
After upgrading to 3.29.0, running the web application in debug mode requires downloading more than 90MB of static resources, and my browser is stuck and cannot be used.

This issue must be addressed before HTML Renderer deprecation.

Chrome > Menu > [CHECK] Desktop mode

Canvas kit -> Doesn't render anything in Desktop mode web mobile, always hang up on index.html blank screen.

Html Renderer -> Works perfectly.

In my opinion, communication with the community needs to be drastically improved.
The breaking changes in the 3.29 [release notes](https://docs.flutter.dev/release/breaking-changes#released-in-flutter-3-29) do not mention that the web-renderer option has been removed.

Invoking `flutter build web --help` now tells me that this version got the `--wasm` option, it does not include the `no-wasm` option however. Why is that? That option has been mentioned here: https://docs.flutter.dev/platform-integration/web/renderers

My [question](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/145954#issuecomment-2559602422) on those options still remains unanswered.

@higaski 

> Could someone please explain the naming of the new web renderers to me? Why does the documentation call them **default** and **WebAssembly**? To me it looks like all new renderers are WebAssembly based?
> 
> There is
> 
> * `canvaskit.wasm`
> * `chromium/canvaskit.wasm`
> * `skwasm.wasm`
> 
> Isn't `.wasm` the WebAssembly extension?
> 
> Also, please make sure to **not** repeat issue [#96509](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/96509). A user picking the `--no-wasm` option does not want the additional garbage collected stuff...

We have one renderer: canvaskit. BUT, we have 3 flavors of canvaskit to allow bindings.

chromium/canvaskit is optimized for Chrome, since Chrome includes features other browsers don't. This allows us to make a smaller binary.

skwasm: this is canvaskit (the name is a portmanteau for skia wasm) built to enable direct wasm-to-wasm binding when you compile your app to wasm.

There is actually a 4th, too!

skwasm_st: is the same as skwasm, but built for single-threaded execution.

We'd REALLY like to have fewer flavors for everyone's sanity, but rest assured these are all canvaskit.
@PrathivDev – feel free to reach out to me on socials - (links here https://kevmoo.com/) I'd love to understand your issues more!

please don't remove `Html Renderer`, just leave it as is with a note like stop improving, at least that way there will be an option for people who have `CORS` problem _(ex. show image assets from open API url)_ or experienced Html smoother than Canvaskit with their specific product (I'm in both cases)

> [@higaski](https://github.com/higaski)
> 
> > Could someone please explain the naming of the new web renderers to me? Why does the documentation call them **default** and **WebAssembly**? To me it looks like all new renderers are WebAssembly based?
> > There is
> > 
> > * `canvaskit.wasm`
> > * `chromium/canvaskit.wasm`
> > * `skwasm.wasm`
> > 
> > Isn't `.wasm` the WebAssembly extension?
> > Also, please make sure to **not** repeat issue [#96509](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/96509). A user picking the `--no-wasm` option does not want the additional garbage collected stuff...
> 
> We have one renderer: canvaskit. BUT, we have 3 flavors of canvaskit to allow bindings.
> 
> chromium/canvaskit is optimized for Chrome, since Chrome includes features other browsers don't. This allows us to make a smaller binary.
> 
> skwasm: this is canvaskit (the name is a portmanteau for skia wasm) built to enable direct wasm-to-wasm binding when you compile your app to wasm.
> 
> There is actually a 4th, too!
> 
> skwasm_st: is the same as skwasm, but built for single-threaded execution.
> 
> We'd REALLY like to have fewer flavors for everyone's sanity, but rest assured these are all canvaskit.

Thank you for the explanation. Now I know this is a little like reading coffee residue but are you expecting the .wasm version which works on all browsers to drop in size?
Currently my web app (working on all major browsers) has increased in size from 1.1 to 3.8 MB just by switching to canvaskit.

In my case,
web app need to load **21.1MB** static files using `flutter build web`.
while it needs only **5.2MB** static files using `flutter build web --web-renderer html`.

I do not understand why you removed the html renderer.

@2000calories - please see the explanation in the bug description ☝.

The size delta should only be about 1MB...unless the rest of it is fonts. Is that what you're seeing?

> [@2000calories](https://github.com/2000calories) - please see the explanation in the bug description ☝.
> 
> The size delta should only be about 1MB...unless the rest of it is fonts. Is that what you're seeing?

That's only true if you're talking about Chromium (and therefor the canvaskit implementation inside the `chromium` subdirectory) exclusively.
For non-Chromium it's about ~2MB compressed if I remember correctly.
